title: The Personal OS gains a local companion app — amending the zero-custom-code constitution
context: The architecture's founding constraint (AD-1, G5) is zero custom code and zero infrastructure: the system is markdown files interpreted by a VS Code AI assistant. Three M6 objectives break that ceiling: (1) the static onboarding.html gives a new user no guided path to an initialised constitution; (2) daily rituals (standup, backlog processing, wrap-up) are invisible — there is no surface showing OS state, staleness, or what the AI harvested; (3) the user wants npm run onboard and npm run dev entry points modelled on the claude-agents cockpit. Any interactive experience that reads live file state and collects structured human input requires running code. The question is where that code runs and how much of the constitution it displaces.

options.label: A
options.summary: Stay zero-code: improve the static onboarding.html and keep all rituals chat-only
pros: Preserves G5 (zero custom code, zero infrastructure) and non-technical maintainability untouched
No Node.js prerequisite, no dependency tree, nothing to keep patched in the template
Nothing new to explain in onboarding — the repo stays 'just files'
cons: A static page cannot read live OS state, so it can never show real staleness, priorities, or backlog contents
No structured input collection — onboarding stays 'read this page, then go type things at the AI', which is exactly the guidance gap being complained about
Fails all three M6 objectives; the improvement is cosmetic

options.label: B
options.summary: Ship a local-only Work HQ app inside the template, run via npm scripts; additive, never load-bearing
pros: Meets all three objectives: guided onboarding wizard, visual ritual screens, npm run onboard / npm run dev entry points
Local process with direct workspace file access — can render markdown OS state live and persist human input, with zero hosting and zero recurring cost
Degrades cleanly: extends AD-4's additive principle — every ritual keeps working chat-only if the app is never started, so the constitution's availability guarantees survive intact
Proven pattern exists (claude-agents cockpit) to fork rather than invent
cons: The template now ships code: a dependency tree to maintain, upgrades to manage, and a class of bugs the markdown-only OS could not have
Adds a Node.js ≥ 20 prerequisite and ~250 MB of node_modules for users who opt in (see OQ-002)
Widens the gap between 'non-technical user maintains their own context' (G2, still true) and 'non-technical user maintains the tooling' (now false — tooling is maintained upstream in the template)

options.label: C
options.summary: Hosted web app (e.g. Vercel) serving the Work HQ as a URL
pros: Zero local setup for the user — no Node, no npm install
One always-current deployment instead of per-clone copies
cons: The entire persistence layer is local markdown in the user's workspace — a hosted app cannot read or write it without a sync agent, which is far more infrastructure than a local dev server
Personal, gitignored content (me.md, active.md, learnings) would transit a third-party host: a direct AD-5 data-governance breach
Introduces hosting cost, auth, and an operations burden — the exact things AD-1 exists to avoid

decision: Adopt Option B. The Personal OS gains a local-only companion app ('Work HQ') living inside the template repo, started with npm run dev (daily Work HQ) and npm run onboard (guided first-run wizard, ADR-P6-004). The constitution is amended, not repealed: AD-1 still holds for the OS itself — all durable state remains plain markdown files and every ritual remains fully operable through chat alone with the app never started. The Work HQ is classified exactly like MCP under AD-4: additive, never load-bearing. It renders OS state and collects human input, but the AI assistant remains the only writer of constitution-level files and the markdown files remain the single source of truth (ADR-P6-002). architecture.md gains a new decision (AD-9) recording this amendment once accepted.
consequences: The template carries a code directory with its own package.json, lockfile, and upgrade cadence — maintained upstream in the template, not by individual users (their git pull picks up Work HQ fixes like it picks up skill fixes). The Node prerequisite is defused per OQ-002 (resolved): the README is written to be AI-assistant-friendly — the non-technical user points their assistant at it and the assistant executes the prerequisites (Node.js install, npm install, first launch) on their behalf; the chat-only path stays fully supported as the fallback. NFR table gains a row: every ritual passes with the Work HQ not running. Forecloses nothing: the app can be deleted from a clone and the OS still works.
costCapacity: No new recurring or metered cost. The app runs on localhost only; no hosting, no external API calls, no CI additions in this phase. One-time local disk cost ~250 MB node_modules per clone that opts in; npm install network cost on first run only.
